mnemonics.title: SIMD Core Idea: Single Instructor, Many Dancers
mnemonics.content: One instructor (Control Unit) shouts a move (Instruction), and all dancers (Processing Elements) perform the same move at the same time, in their own personal space (on their own Data).

mnemonics.title: Classical vs Modern SIMD
mnemonics.content: Classical = Processor Arrays (ILLIAC IV, CM-2: thousands of PEs). Modern = Packed SIMD in CPUs (AVX, NEON: one core, wide registers). GPU = SIMT (warps of 32 threads). All share the 'one instruction, many data' principle.

mnemonics.title: SIMD Branching Problem: M.A.S.K.
mnemonics.content: Masking Active PEs, Some idle, Killing efficiency. When if-else occurs, all PEs execute both paths but mask (disable) results for PEs where the condition is false. The more divergent the branches, the worse the performance.

mnemonics.title: SIMD Advantages: T.E.S.
mnemonics.content: Throughput (high for data-parallel tasks), Energy efficient (one CU for many ALUs), Synchronization is free (lockstep means no explicit locks needed).

mnemonics.title: AVX Register Width Evolution
mnemonics.content: MMX=64-bit, SSE=128-bit, AVX=256-bit, AVX-512=512-bit. Each generation doubles the width, doubling the data elements processed per instruction. Think: 64 -> 128 -> 256 -> 512 (powers of 2 progression).
